sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 94, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 68 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 moved the dark gray shape from rows 39-50 to rows 36-47 (up by 3 rows). DOWN didn't work after ACTION7.
So ACTION7 seems to move the shape up by 3 rows (same as UP).
Let me reconsider the problem. I've been trying various positions and actions, but the level hasn't completed. Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. Moving the dark gray shape to the top of the left panel
2. Overlapping the dark gray shape with the yellow shape
3. Changing the dot color to a specific value when the shape is at a specific position
Looking at the image, the dots on the black shape are now sky blue (after ACTION7). Let me check if the dots changed color.
Actually, looking at the image, the dots appear to be sky blue. Let me try pressing SPACE to change them to white, and see if the level completes.
But I've tried SPACE many times at various positions and it hasn't completed the level.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the dark gray shape to overlap with the yellow shape, and the overlap creates a new pattern that completes the level.
Currently:
- Dark gray: rows 36-47, cols 12-26
- Yellow: rows 42-53, cols 3-17
They overlap at rows 42-47, cols 12-17.
To fully overlap, I need the dark gray shape at rows 42-53, cols 3-17.
From rows 36-47, I need to move down 6 rows and left 9 cols.
But DOWN doesn't seem to work at many positions. Let me try UP to move to a position where DOWN works, then navigate to the target.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me check if the dark gray shape can be moved to the TOP of the panel (rows 18-29), where I know UP/DOWN work.
From rows 36-47, UP moves to rows 33-44, then 30-41, then 27-38, then 24-35, then 21-32, then 18-29.
At rows 18-29, I can try LEFT/RIGHT to move to cols 3-17, then DOWN to rows 42-53.
But this would take many actions, and I'm already over the action budget.
Let me try a shorter sequence: move to rows 42-53, cols 3-17 directly.
From rows 36-47, cols 12-26:
- DOWN: doesn't work (tried before)
- UP: moves to rows 33-44
- LEFT: ?
- RIGHT: ?
Let me try LEFT at rows 36-47.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: dark gray at rows 36-47, cols 12-26
# Try LEFT and RIGHT to see movement
action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray after LEFT: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Dark gray after LEFT: row 36-47, col 6-20
Level completed: False
